text: A fifty-four-year-old female with history of open duodenal switch, seventeen years ago, presented with severe gastroesophageal reflux, heartburn, abdominal fullness as an occasional water brush despite therapy with ranitidine and pantoprazole daily.
Also, the patient underwent an open cholecystectomy in nineteen eighty-six and an open incisional hernia repair in two thousand fourteen.
Before the duodenal switch, her BMI was fifty four point eight, which decreased to forty one point nine over the first postoperative year.
At present, her BMI is forty six point six.
Preoperative studies were performed.
An EGT showed great ED esophagitis.
An upper gastrointestinal contrast study, a stricture of the duodenal jejunostomy and gastroparesis, and pH monitoring evidenced at the MRS score of seventy-seven point two.
Decision was made to undergo a robotic assisted conversion from duodenal switch to run Y gastric bypass.
The surgeon begins the procedure by inserting a 5mm trocar into the left upper quadrant using the OptiView technique.
The diagnostic laparoscopy reveals multiple adhesions between the small bowel and the abdominal wall, omentum, and colon.
Because the patient had also a previous open repair of incisional hernia with mesh, there were multiple additions between the omentum to the abdominal wall.
The surgeon carefully dissects and detaches the omentum from the abdominal wall, colon, and small bowel, separating these structures with precision.
The surgeon carefully dissects the dilated stomach, separating the omentum from the shoulder line of the sleeve gastrectomy.
The surgeon dissects the hiatus, separating it from surrounding tissues using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon creates a retroesophageal window by dissecting the right and left crura, revealing the anatomical space behind the esophagus.
The surgeon passes a Penrose drain around the esophagus and performs a circumferential dissection of the esophagus, extending approximately ten centimeters into the mediastinum.
Approximately three centimeters of the esophagus is retracted back into the abdominal cavity.
After completing the dissection of the lateral aspect of the sleeve, the surgeon proceeds to dissect into the small bowel, carefully separating the tissue layers.
The patient had a retrocholic alimentary limb.
The surgeon frees the small bowel from the colon and follows it into the supracolonic area, maneuvering the alimentary limb back into the supracolic region.
The surgeon accesses the lesser curvature of the stomach and uses a robotic vessel sealer to take down additional tissue attachments.
The surgeon dissects and takes down the right gastric artery and the pyloric artery, removing these vessels from their attachments.
The surgeon transects the small bowel just inferior to the duodenal jejunostomy after the sleeve gastrectomy is fully immobilized.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the mesenterium, followed by the division of the small bowel using an endo-GIA tan load stapler.
The surgeon creates the gastric pouch approximately five centimeters from the G-injection site using an endo-GIA black load-sixty stapler.
A 40-French bougie is inserted into the gastric pouch to guide and tailor its creation, ensuring the desired size and shape.
The surgeon places the remnant distal stomach and the duodenal jejunostomy into an endopouch and retrieves them through a fifteen millimeter trocar.
The surgeon creates a new gastrojejunostomy in two layers, visually connecting the gastric pouch to the jejunum.
The surgeon completes the posterior layer of the gastrojejunostomy using 3-0 PDS sutures in a running fashion. The inner and outer layers are also constructed with running 3-0 PDS sutures.
The surgeon repairs the hiatal hernia using interrupted sutures with 0 Ethibond, securing the tissue at the site of the hernia.
The surgeon reattaches the omentum to the lateral suture line, securing it in place.
The surgeon adjusts the position of the pouch to release tension and stabilize it within the surgical field.
An intraoperative endoscopy reveals a wood anastomosis and a small gastric pouch, with no visible evidence of leaks.
The operative room time was two hundred twenty five minutes.
The postoperative upper GI showed no evidence of leaks and the patient was asymptomatic and she was discharged in postoperative day four.
